content.text: When React updates the DOM, it needs to figure out which elements have changed, which are new, and which should be removed. For lists, this comparison is called
prop is how React identifies each list item across renders — without it, React falls back to position-based matching, which produces incorrect results whenever the list order changes.

attrs.alt: Side-by-side: index-based matching loses input state; key-based matching preserves it
attrs.title: Without keys React reuses the wrong element; with keys it correctly identifies each item

content.text: On every render, React compares the new virtual DOM tree with the previous one (the "diffing" step). For a list of children, React needs a strategy to match old nodes to new nodes:

React matches by position. The element at index 0 before the update is assumed to be the same element at index 0 after. If you prepend an item, every element shifts position and React updates all of them rather than inserting one.
React matches by key value. If

existed before and still exists after, React knows those two virtual nodes represent the same element. It moves the real DOM node, preserving any local state (focus, scroll, form input values) inside that element.

The bug is clearest when list items contain input state. If a user types into a list item and you then prepend a new item, React matches the new item to the old DOM node at index 0 and assigns the previously typed value to the new item:
When you click "Prepend" after typing in Alice's input, the typed text appears under "New" — React reused Alice's DOM input node for the first position.
What React preserves when it matches a key
When React finds the same key in both the old and new trees, it
reuses the existing component instance and DOM node
. This means all of the following are preserved across the re-render:
Uncontrolled form input values (text, checkboxes, selects with
DOM element properties like scroll position, focus, and selection
a key on a component, React unmounts the old instance and mounts a fresh one, resetting all state. This is a useful technique for forced resets (e.g.
starts fresh when the user changes).
Performance: reconciliation cost with vs without keys

When a list item moves (due to prepend, reorder, or sort), React with keys moves the real DOM node — an inexpensive operation. Without keys, React destroys the old DOM node and creates a new one at each position — more expensive and causes visual resets. For a list of 1000 items where only one changes, the difference is significant.
Correct (indices unchanged); creates one new node
Updates ALL existing nodes; creates one new node
Moves existing nodes; creates one new node
Index-as-key is safe only when all three of these are true simultaneously:
— items are never added, removed, or reordered after the initial render.
, uncontrolled inputs, focus, or scroll position to preserve.
from data (e.g. rendering an array of strings like
If any condition fails, use a stable ID. When the data doesn't include one, generate a stable ID at the time the data enters state (not inside the render function).
Where the key must be placed
must go on the outermost element returned by the
callback — not on a child element inside it. React looks for keys at the sibling level, not deeper inside the tree.

Using key to force component reset
Because changing a key causes React to unmount the old instance and mount a fresh one, you can intentionally reset a component's state by changing its key. This is cleaner than
When data doesn't include an ID, generate one when the data enters state — not inside the render function (generating IDs in render means a new ID on every render, defeating the purpose):
is consumed by React and is
. If you need the ID inside the component, pass it explicitly as a separate prop.
Keys only need to be unique among siblings within the same list. The same key value can appear in different lists without conflict.
is never rendered to the DOM. It exists only in React's virtual DOM.
React emits a warning without keys and falls back to fragile index-based matching.
Test the behavior that keys enable — that existing items retain state when the list changes — rather than testing the key value directly:

CSS transition libraries like Framer Motion and React Spring observe key changes to animate elements in and out. When a keyed element is removed from the tree, the library can play an exit animation before React removes the DOM node. When a new key appears, an entrance animation plays. This only works because stable keys let the library distinguish "same element moving" from "old element gone, new element arrived".
React logs a warning to the browser console when a list element is missing a key:

Warning: Each child in a list should have a unique "key" prop.
Check the render method of `TodoList`.
To locate the component, look at the component name in the warning. Common places where keys are missed:
is missing a key even when the outer one has it.
When a list item suspends (e.g. it lazy-loads a component or fetches data), React shows the nearest

fallback and re-mounts the item when it resolves. Keys still identify which item is which, so other items in the list are not affected by one item's suspension. Without stable keys, suspension resolution can re-mount the wrong item.
Common patterns for generating stable keys
Reconciliation deep dive: the diffing algorithm
React's reconciler (Fiber) uses a heuristic algorithm to compare two virtual DOM trees in O(n) time rather than O(n³). The algorithm has two key assumptions that make this possible:
), React destroys the old subtree and builds a fresh one. It never tries to reconcile children across a type change.
Keys hint which child is the same across renders:
Within a list of children of the same parent, React uses keys to match old children to new children before deciding what to create, update, or destroy.

Without keys, React's list algorithm runs left-to-right: it checks if old[0] and new[0] are the same type, then old[1] vs new[1], and so on. Prepending an item shifts every index, so React sees every element as changed. With keys, React builds a map of
from the old children and then looks up each new key, moving or updating the matched fiber if found, or creating a new one if not.
What happens to component lifecycle during reconciliation
The consequence of key-based matching on component lifecycle methods (and hooks) is significant. Here is what each reconciliation outcome triggers:
Same key, same type, updated props
Updates the existing DOM node / fiber
Unmounts the component; removes DOM node
Mounts a fresh component; creates DOM node
Cleanup on old, setup on new; all state lost
Same key, same type, same props

Drag-and-drop list reordering is a concrete case where wrong keys break everything. Without stable keys, React destroys and recreates every DOM node during reorder, losing event listeners, CSS transitions, and scroll positions. With stable keys, React moves the real DOM nodes and the reorder animation is smooth:
to match list elements across renders; without it, matching falls back to position.
Index-as-key causes state bugs whenever items are prepended, removed, or reordered.
When React matches a key, it reuses the existing DOM node and component instance, preserving state and scroll position.
Changing a key forces a full remount, which is a useful technique for resetting component state.

) when multiple sibling elements are returned.
Generate stable IDs when data is created, not inside the render function.
